title: Information, Accountability, and Corruption: Evidence from Random Audits in Brazil

qa.question: What is the mean share of resources with audit irregularities?
qa.answer: 0.082 (8.2 percent)

qa.question: How many municipalities are in the sample?
What fraction of municipalities have a local newspaper?
qa.answer: 0.34 (34 percent)

qa.question: What is the mean number of local radio stations?

tables.caption: Effect of Audit Disclosure on Electoral Accountability
tables.label: tab:main-results

qa.question: What is the main effect of audit disclosure on incumbent vote share?
qa.answer: -0.041 (4.1 percentage point decrease) in column 1

qa.question: What is the interaction effect of disclosure and irregularity share?
qa.answer: -0.084 in column 3, indicating larger electoral penalties in more corrupt municipalities

qa.question: What controls are included in column 5?
qa.answer: Municipality controls and state fixed effects

qa.question: How are standard errors clustered?
qa.answer: At the state level

tables.caption: Effect of Audit Disclosure on Re-Election Probability
tables.label: tab:reelection

qa.question: Does audit disclosure reduce re-election of corrupt mayors?
qa.answer: Yes, by 9.8 percentage points for high-irregularity mayors (column 1)

qa.question: Does audit disclosure affect re-election of non-corrupt mayors?
qa.answer: No, the effect for low-irregularity mayors is -0.012 and insignificant

qa.question: What is the control mean re-election rate for high-irregularity mayors?
qa.answer: 0.38 (38 percent)

tables.caption: Media Exposure and the Electoral Effect of Audits
tables.label: tab:media-interaction

qa.question: Is the electoral penalty from audits larger in municipalities with local radio?
qa.answer: Yes, the triple interaction is -0.058 (significant at 1% level)

qa.question: Is the electoral penalty from audits larger in municipalities with newspapers?
qa.answer: Yes, the triple interaction is -0.071 (significant at 1% level)

qa.question: Is there an electoral penalty in municipalities without media?
qa.answer: The base interaction is -0.024, small and insignificant, suggesting minimal penalty without media

qa.question: What is the RD estimate of audit disclosure on vote share?
qa.answer: -0.038 with the local linear estimator

qa.question: Does audit disclosure increase voter turnout?
qa.answer: Yes, by 2.4 percentage points (local linear)

qa.question: Does audit disclosure increase challenger entry?
qa.answer: Yes, by 21.8 percentage points

qa.question: Does audit disclosure increase radio coverage?
qa.answer: Yes, by 41.2 percentage points

qa.question: Does disclosure increase voter awareness of corruption?
qa.answer: Yes, by 34.2 percentage points

qa.question: What is the control mean of voter awareness?
qa.answer: 0.18 (18 percent)

tables.caption: Effect of Audit Disclosure on Subsequent Corruption
tables.label: tab:corruption-outcomes

qa.question: Does audit disclosure reduce subsequent corruption?
qa.answer: Yes, by 2.8 percentage points of federal transfers (column 1)

qa.question: Is the persistence of corruption significant?
qa.answer: Yes, prior irregularity share coefficient is 0.384 (highly persistent)

qa.question: Is the deterrence effect larger for more corrupt municipalities?
qa.answer: Yes, the interaction is -0.148, indicating stronger deterrence where prior corruption was higher

qa.question: Are results robust to dropping top 5% outliers?
qa.answer: Yes, the coefficient is -0.081 (slightly larger), significant at 1%

qa.question: Are results robust to ordered probit?
qa.answer: Yes, the coefficient is -0.068, significant at 1%

qa.question: Do placebo tests reject the null?
qa.answer: No, all coefficients are small and insignificant, supporting the identification strategy

tables.caption: Appendix: Audit Lottery Selection and Balance
tables.label: tab:appendix-audit

qa.question: Is the audit lottery random?
qa.answer: Yes, the joint F-test p-value is 0.78, confirming balance

equations.label: eq:voting
equations.description: Voting model: $v_i^*$ is incumbent vote share, $D_i$ is disclosure indicator, $C_i$ is corruption measure, and the interaction $D_i \times C_i$ captures information-dependent accountability.

qa.question: What does the interaction term D_i x C_i capture?
qa.answer: The additional electoral penalty for corruption that occurs when voters are informed by audit disclosure

equations.label: eq:bayesian
equations.description: Bayesian updating: voter posterior belief about incumbent type ($\pi_H$ = honest) given audit signal $s_i$.

equations.label: eq:optimal-vote
equations.description: Optimal voting rule: re-elect the incumbent iff the posterior probability of honesty exceeds a threshold determined by the relative payoffs.

equations.label: eq:accountability
equations.description: Accountability condition: when audits are disclosed ($D=1$), the electoral penalty for corruption is $\gamma + \delta$. The key prediction is $\delta < 0$.

equations.label: eq:deterrence
equations.description: Official's optimal corruption $C^*$: balances rent extraction $R(C)$, legal penalty $L(C)$, and electoral sanction $\Delta v(C)$ weighted by audit probability and value of office $V$.

qa.question: What determines the optimal level of corruption?
qa.answer: The tradeoff between rents from corruption, legal penalties, and electoral sanctions conditional on audit probability

equations.label: eq:rd
equations.latex: v_i = \alpha + \tau \cdot \mathbf{1}(d_i > 0) + f(d_i) + X_i'\gamma + \varepsilon_i, \quad d_i = \text{election date} - \text{audit release date}
equations.description: Regression discontinuity: $d_i$ is days between audit release and election. Treatment: audit disclosed before election ($d_i > 0$). $f(d_i)$ is local polynomial.

equations.label: eq:media-channel
equations.latex: v_i^* = \mu + \beta D_i + \gamma C_i + \delta_1 (D_i \times C_i \times M_i) + \delta_0 (D_i \times C_i \times (1-M_i)) + X_i'\theta + \varepsilon_i
equations.description: Extended model decomposing accountability by media presence $M_i$. Tests whether electoral punishment requires media to inform voters.

equations.label: eq:voi
equations.description: Value of information (VOI): expected gain from making informed decisions (conditional on signal $s$) minus the value of the best uninformed decision.

equations.label: eq:persistence
equations.description: Persistence of corruption: $\rho$ captures baseline persistence; $\phi$ measures the deterrence effect of disclosure on future corruption.

equations.label: eq:welfare
equations.latex: \Delta W = \underbrace{\Pr(\text{high type elected} | D=1) - \Pr(\text{high type elected} | D=0)}_{\text{selection effect}} \times (V_H - V_L)
equations.description: Welfare gain from audit disclosure: improved selection of honest politicians times the value difference between high and low types.

qa.question: What is the main identification strategy?
qa.answer: Random audit lottery combined with quasi-random timing of audit disclosure relative to elections

qa.question: What is the main effect of audit disclosure on incumbent vote share?
qa.answer: -4.1 percentage points (ITT)

qa.question: How does media amplify the accountability effect?
qa.answer: The triple interaction of disclosure, corruption, and radio presence adds -5.8 percentage points

qa.question: Does disclosure reduce future corruption?
qa.answer: Yes, by 2.8 percentage points in subsequent audits

qa.question: How many municipalities are in the sample?
qa.answer: 1,891 audited municipalities, 1,456 with incumbent re-election data
